sentences.text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]
So we went to Berchtesgaden in Obersalzberg, where Hitler had his-- his home and where he dispatched a lot of business from there, too.
So it was-- it was pretty much damaged.
Not totally destroyed, but it was damaged by-- by bombings, by British bombings I was told later on.
And we went in.
Of course, we were afraid of what they called booby traps at the time.
Booby traps are little bombs or explosives attached to a wire.
You pull a door and it explodes, or you step on it.
So when we went in there, we went in there very cautiously.
And we couldn't believe it.
I mean, I couldn't believe it.
I had come from Germany just 40 months earlier.
You know-- and here I was in his-- in his-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] office, in his private library with that huge-- famous window, looking out the mountains.
There was no glass in there anymore, of course.
And a huge, beautiful desk standing there with a chair.
And we wanted to open the desk, but we were afraid of booby traps.
So we attached a wire to the knob of the main door.
And we went outside.
We're lying down, and then we pulled-- you know.
Nothing happened.
So we went inside.
And there was nothing in there, but about 20 sheets of his personal stationery.
Of Hitler's personal stationery?
Hitler's personal stationery.
And I still have one or two of those sheets.
I have it with me.
I can show it to you later on.
Well, at that time, I mean, I didn't even know yet about the destruction of the Jews, see?
I mean, at that time, to me, Hitler was fighting a-- a war against the Allies.
He-- he behaved extremely bad against the Jews, but I did not know yet at that particular moment of-- of the Holocaust.
I knew that many Jews were killed by the Germans.
But I did not know, I didn't realize the extent of it.
It was just unimaginable.
So to get to this, let me go back a little bit again before the war was over.
I am talking now about April 1945, exactly to be three weeks before the war was over, yes, or four weeks before the war was over.
The war was over on May the 8th.
I went about on-- on April the 15th-- around April the 15th, I went from where we were at the time-- at the time, we were in Southern Germany.
And I always had told my commanding officer, the first chance we get, please let me go to my hometown.
So he said-- and I always was nagging and nagging and nagging, you know?
And I-- so I asked him.
I said, listen now-- look, I mean-- it's-- I mean, how long can this war last, you know?
I mean, we were right across Germany.
And he said, all right.
He said, all right, you asked for it.
But you're on your own.
I'll give you a seven-day pass.
Is that good enough?
I said, yes.
Thank you very much.
He-- he said that-- take everything you want, take it with you.
It will be your own responsibility.
Take the Jeep-- he gave me the Heep.
And I loaded it up with gasoline, with these huge gasoline the army had.
And I mounted a-- a .30 millimeter machine gun on it.
I had the-- with myself, I had the Thompson submachine gun.
And I had a .45 pistol.
And took plenty of K-rations along.
The K-rations was what we ate.
There was a K-ration for breakfast, one for lunch, and one for dinner.
The K-ration even had toilet paper in it.
So I was packed with K-rations, with gasoline, and a map-- which the G2, which was the intelligence unit of the division, to which I pertained.
They marked the latest frontlines because they were moving every day, what I would have to watch out for, et cetera, et cetera.
And we said goodbye.
And I couldn't go to Stuttgart directly.
I had to go all the way around because they were still fighting in Heilbronn, a town about 80 miles north of Stuttgart.
And, well, I find-- many bridges were blown up.
So I had to see how to get on the pontoon bridges I went across.
I finally made it to Stuttgart.
And when I got to Stuttgart, somehow I thought Stuttgart should look the same when I left it.
But it certainly didn't.
It was one huge rubble.
I mean, you couldn't even-- you couldn't even see the streets.
I'm talking about the center of the city now where I came in.
I came in where the railroad station was, and what used to be the main street.
It was just one rubble.
The streetcars were all burned out.
The buildings were burned out or totally torn down by bomb damage.
And what about your house on the marktplatz?
Well, the house on the marktplatz did not stand anymore.
And you didn't know what had--
In fact, most houses on the markplatz didn't stand anymore.
The [NON-ENGLISH] or the-- the town hall was completely burned out.
I-- I didn't even know sometime where I was.
So I figured-- I came to myself.
I said, now where do you go to?
So I went to where we used to live-- I used to live with my parents before they went to-- to Czechoslovakia, you know.
That was in [NON-ENGLISH], 39B.
And there, the house was complete-- no damage.
And when I was there, I remembered-- I looked up where we used to live.
The linen curtains.
There were other curtains there.
And across the street, there was a friend of mine who used to live there.
His name was Ludwig [NON-ENGLISH].
His father was the director of the [NON-ENGLISH], of the brewery-- of the beer.
And he was-- we were good friends until 1935, '36, and then he didn't look at me anymore.
So I went there.
That house was still up, too.
And there was a big, wooden door.
It was an apartment house, four or five storeys high.
I rang the bell.
Nothing happened.
And then I saw the door was-- you could open the door.
I opened the door.
I went up to the first floor.
I rang the bell.
And Ludwig [NON-ENGLISH] mother opened the door.
She was clad all in black.
I didn't like that.
And she looked at me.
I took my steel helmet off, you know-- and she looked at me.
And she looked at the uniform.
And she said, Richard?
I mean, she had seen me maybe in-- the last time in 19-- probably '36 or '37. '36 I would say.
So that was eight or nine years later.
And she made it as a question mark, you know, Richard?
Richard?
Richard Pick?
I said [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes, Mrs.
[NON-ENGLISH].
She said, come in.
I came in.
She told me that Ludwig died for Fuhrer [NON-ENGLISH].
And he had also a sister.
His sister came in.
I knew the sister as a kid.
She came in, she was also glad-- they had just received the-- the notification shortly before.
I mean, a few days or a week before.
So I didn't feel too good.
I mean, I came there as a soldier.
And then they said to me, in uniform?
You know, because in Stuttgart, there were not US soldiers-- I think I was the first US soldier in Stuttgart, because Stuttgart was conquered by the French army.
And there were only French soldiers in Stuttgart at the time.
How did they treat you?
The [NON-ENGLISH]?
Well, I mean, they treated me well, but cool.
Yes.
And I felt uneasy.
I felt very uneasy.
And I think I was not there for longer than maybe 15 or 20 minutes.
And I said goodbye.
I said, I have to go back, you know.
I said goodbye.
In fact, we were sitting there and we could have told each other so much.
So much, you know?
But they couldn't speak.
And I couldn't speak.
Just too much had happened.
So I said goodbye.
And I was on the street again-- my Jeep.
Now, what do I do?
So I thought off a fellow who was half-Jewish.
His father was Jewish.
And he lived in the suburb called [NON-ENGLISH]-- which belonged to Stuttgart, but a little bit outside.
And I went there, but I didn't know the address.
I was driving around.
There was hardly any destruction there.
They had all the little gardens, the little houses.
And I asked-- the family's name was Lipster.
His name was Alfred Lipster.
He was a very good, close friend of mine.
And I asked for Lipster-- Lipster family, four or five times.
And then all of a sudden, the woman told me, yes, they live just right over the corner there-- over there.
I went there, rang the bell.
Mrs.
Lipster came out.
And we-- hello.
And everything was beautiful.
His father was taken to concentration camp, too.
They didn't have any idea where he was.
And I said, where is Alfred?
And she said, he's at the garden there in the back.
The flower garden and vegetables.
You know, they didn't have anything to eat, so they grew their own vegetables.
So I went there.
There was Alfred.
And of course, we put the arms around each other.
And he had been in a-- in the-- the little prisoner camp just outside of Stuttgart, but it was nothing too serious.
And he was there for a couple of months, and then he was discharged and he could go back home.
I said, what do you know-- and it was my first question.
What do you know about my family?
He said, listen.
I don't know anything about it.
But I have heard that many Jews have been killed in concentration camps.
There are all kinds of stories.
I have heard, I don't want to paint it too bleak for you.
But if I would be you, I wouldn't have too many hopes that you will see your parents again.
That really hit me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was actually the first time that someone who was close to me told me this.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I said to-- I gave him K-rations.
I said, listen-- next day, I'm going to go back to my unit, you know.
I left him K-rations.
I left him oranges-- he hadn't seen oranges in many, many years.
And he said, listen.
I also want to go to the United States when this war is over.
Give me your address.
I said, listen, Alfred.
When I get back to the States, when I get back to New York, I don't even know where I'm going to live.
I lived in a room.
I don't know where I'm going to live.
But I can give you my APO number.
You know, we all had APO numbers in the army.
So I gave him my APO number, which was 403.
And he put that down.
And I said goodbye, and goodbye to his mother.
And we went back and the war was over.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I was transferred into the Seventh Army-- from the Third Army into the Seventh Army Interrogation Center, because the-- the biggest interrogation center of the US army was the Seventh Army Interrogation Center, which was created to interrogate German big shots.
I was transferred to the Seventh Army Interrogation Center, which at the time the war was over with Germany.
And I came to Augsburg, Seventh Army Interrogation Center-- it must have been in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the end of May or the beginning of June I would say.
Oh, yes.
We-- the Seventh Army Interrogation Center-- I never knew exactly because all of this was being kept a secret, even from us.
But I would estimate that when I got there, there were maybe-- I would say maybe 50 or 60 Germans there already, which was actually about 6 weeks after the war.
They were brought.
There were instructions to all divisions and to all the armies that every minister or any big shot, any general should be brought to that Seventh Army Interrogation Center.
So when I got to the Seventh Army Interrogation Center, there was a good crowd.
At that time, there was already-- I remember there was Dr.
Otto Zorge-- he was there already.
He was the German post minister.
There was [NON-ENGLISH], who made the Olympic film of 1936.
She was a very famous movie actress also, and the film producer.
While I was in Seventh Army Interrogation Center, maybe after two or three days, Goering was brought in.
Goering was the second in command after Hitler.
He was a big shot.
I mean, he was-- after Hitler, he was the number one man.
He was brought in.
I took photos.
I have photos here.
I took photos of him when he-- as he came out of his car.
And well, our time was spent with interrogations.
At the time, we didn't have any-- any-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] modern equipment to tape-- no tape recordings.
We had records, you now, with the needles.
And with earphones, they were sitting there.
They were connected with wires, you know, to the rooms where they had microphones in there in the rooms where the prisoners lived, you know, to overhear what they were saying.
So we took the prisoners out to walk.
I had four or five which I had to take out for a one-hour walk in the morning, one-hour walk in the afternoon.
And then about four weeks later or so, the whole Seventh Army Interrogation Center was transferred to [NON-ENGLISH], which is between Heidelberg and Mannheim.
And there, we established outside.
It was a huge convoy with all kinds of guard units accompanying us.
And I was sitting in my jeep, actually with Dr.
Otto Zorge, he was in my Jeep.
And he's a-- he's a [NON-ENGLISH], he was a major in the German army.
And we were driving in the convoy to [NON-ENGLISH], and then it was unloaded by Puerto Ricans.
We had Puerto Rican soldiers there.
They were also for the US army.
And we got established there.
After a few days, the interrogations continued.
And this was our daily life.
There was also a Dr.
Kurtz, I want to be a short story.
We also had [NON-ENGLISH] there, who was the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] man in command of the whole German army.
General [NON-ENGLISH] He was, what you would call at the US army, the five star general.
Yes, I do.
Two little story I would like to tell you.
One is-- I played chess once in a while with Dr.
Ottis Zorge, he was a passionate chess player.
He was the post minister, as I mentioned before.
And in fact, he had an edition with him of-- a trial run of the latest German stamps.
I said it must be worth a fortune in the future, I said to him.
He said, it will be worth nothing because they never came officially into circulation.
It was just a trial run which never was put into circulation.
So Dr.
Ottis Zorge, one day, he said, Mr.
Peters-- we all had different names.
My name was Peters-- Richard Peters.
The first name was correct.
Richard Peters.
He said, Mr.
Peters-- he sent me a little note, which I still have, too, in my things-- I would like to talk to you in German.
Please drop by.
So I went over.
He said, Mr.
Peters, I am sorry.
But I would like to ask you.
There is the Streicher.
What was his first name?
Julius.
Julius Streicher.
Julius Streicher.
I heard that Julius Streicher arrived here at the Seventh Army Interrogation Center just a while ago.
And I would like to plead with you, that please that he is not being put into my little apartment building.
There were always six or eight apartments in one building.
And in his building, all the apartments were empty.
His was the only one occupied.
So I said, what is wrong?
I mean, with Mr.
Julius Streicher.
He was one of you people, no?
And he said, how can you-- he said in his best German, how can you compare Julius Streicher with people like me or other people?
He was Dr.
Ottis Zorge-- he had a PhD-- with us?
I mean, I said, how come?
I mean, after all, he was the publisher of the 'Der Sturmer,' which was the most anti-Semitic paper that appeared that was being published in Germany.
A terrible paper, just awful.
And full with lies.
And I said, I mean, what's the problem?
How is this different?
He said, if we-- Julius Streicher, if he's going to [NON-ENGLISH], these are his words.
If he would drink only water for the next two weeks, and he would go out to urinate, his urine still would be 100% alcohol.
Those are his words.
And the other story is when I walked with Dr.
Kurtz.
Dr.
Kurtz, K-U-R-Z or T-Z.
He was the V1 and V2 expert on ballistics.
He was the ballistic expert on the V1 and V2.
The V1 and V2 were rockets.
The first rockets in this world were rockets which were invented by the Germans.
And which were fired-- high-explosive rockets, which were fired into England, into Coventry and London.
They raised a lot of damage.
They really were afraid of those V1's and V2's.
And he was the ballistic expert in [NON-ENGLISH]-- that was where that rocket unit was stationed, near the Baltic Sea in the north of Germany.
And he must have smelled something that I was Jewish.
Because many of the of-- the-- the crew, in the Seventh Army Interrogation Center, I would say maybe 60% was Jewish and 40% was Gentile, but they all spoke German fluently.
Some of them spoke it very well, but most of them fluently.
So he gave me all kinds of speeches about the relativity of the moon to the earth.
What is being fired and the gravity, the pull of the gravity of the moon.
And my goodness.
I mean-- I-- at that time, I never had taken any physics.
I didn't even know what the man was talking about.
So I told my commanding officer.
And of course, he passed it onto higher sources.
And about four or five days later, he was taken away.
And he was shipped to the United States and was working with Wilfred Brown and all these people there in-- in-- where was it?
Someplace in the south of the United States.
So I had a chance to talk to him whenever we walked, the prisoners and I walked, with Dr.
Kurtz.
And he asked me one day.
He said, you know, he said, I was a Nazi.
I belonged to the party.
And I was convinced-- he didn't say Nazi.
He said [NON-ENGLISH], yes.
Nazi is the abbreviation for it.
National Socialist.
But he said, most things I thought were correct, what was being done.
A few things I thought were not so good, but I never opened my mouth.
I must admit that.
He said, but nowadays, when you will be speaking with Germans, you will get the impression, Mr.
Peters, that there were no Nazis in Germany, no National Socialists in Germany.
But there must have been 180 million Jews in Germany.
I say, why 180 million Jews, Dr.
Kurtz?
He said, because every German saved the lives of three Jews.
Since there were 60 million Germans times three, it gives you 180 million Jews.
Of course, there were only about 40,000 Jews in Germany.
But he wanted to convey to me how the Germans changed from one moment to the next.
At least on the outside.
